perturbation.original_text: G) to destroy immediately on our written request all Confidential Information in your possession including any copies made (save where we are required pursuant to some law or enactment or regulation or the rules of any Stock Exchange to retain originals or copies of any of the Confidential Information).
perturbation.changed_text: G) to return immediately on our written request all Confidential Information in your possession (save where we are required pursuant to some law or enactment or regulation or the rules of any Stock Exchange to retain originals or copies of any of the Confidential Information). Destroying copies is optional and at your discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause mandates destruction of all Confidential Information and copies upon written request, with exceptions only for legal/regulatory requirements. The modified clause mandates only the return of information, making the destruction of copies optional. This directly contradicts the initial directive to 'destroy immediately,' creating ambiguity regarding the recipient's obligations. It contradicts the 'keep the Confidential Information secret at all times' since if the copy is still on your possession then it is not being kept secret.
perturbation.contradicted_text: B) to keep the Confidential Information secret at all times
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies clause G, making the destruction of confidential information copies optional instead of mandatory, as it was in the original text. This creates a direct contradiction with clause B, which requires the recipient to 'keep the Confidential Information secret at all times.' If the recipient retains copies, keeping the information secret is undermined, creating a clear conflict between the two clauses.

perturbation.original_text: 6) that the provisions of this agreement shall last for the duration of the discussions relating to the Proposed Transaction and for 2 years following their conclusion.
perturbation.changed_text: 6) that the provisions of this agreement shall last for the duration of the discussions relating to the Proposed Transaction and for 2 years following their conclusion, unless superseded by a subsequent agreement related to the Proposed Transaction, at which point this NDA becomes null and void, regardless of the 2-year period.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces uncertainty. While the original specifies a clear 2-year period after the discussions, the modified version adds a condition that a subsequent agreement can render the NDA void. This creates ambiguity if the subsequent agreement does not explicitly address confidentiality; the parties could dispute whether the NDA's confidentiality provisions still apply. This can contradict the clause A if the agreement is null and void, there is no longer any Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: A) that the Confidential Information shall comprise only written documents explicitly marked as 'Confidential Information' provided directly by us to you in connection with the Proposed Transaction. No oral or electronic information will be considered Confidential Information under this agreement.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a condition where the NDA can become null and void before the original 2-year period, contradicting clause A which defines Confidential Information as written documents explicitly marked as 'Confidential Information'. If the NDA is null and void, as the change suggests, there is no longer a valid definition of Confidential Information which creates a direct contradiction as there is no longer an NDA in effect. The Perturbation Explanation correctly states this as well. Therefore, a strong in-text contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: 7) that these terms shall not apply where the Confidential Information: 7.1 was known to us (by means not importing any requirement of confidentiality or involving a breach of confidentiality by a third party) prior to its provision to us; 7.2 is in or subsequently comes in to the public domain (through no fault on our part); 7.3 is required to be disclosed pursuant to any legal, statutory or regulatory requirement.
perturbation.changed_text: 7) that these terms shall not apply where the Confidential Information: 7.1 was known to you (by means not importing any requirement of confidentiality or involving a breach of confidentiality by a third party) prior to its provision to you; 7.2 is in or subsequently comes in to the public domain (through no fault on our part, unless due to a security vulnerability in your system); 7.3 is required to be disclosed pursuant to any legal, statutory or regulatory requirement.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that the NDA doesn't apply if information becomes public through no fault of the receiving party. The change adds an exception: if the information becomes public due to a security vulnerability in the receiving party's system, the NDA terms *still* apply. This contradicts the general principle established in 7.2. and makes it unclear under what circumstances the receiving party is liable for information entering the public domain. This also can be taken against the rule that states 'that the Confidential Information shall comprise only written documents explicitly marked as 'Confidential Information' provided directly by us to you in connection with the Proposed Transaction. No oral or electronic information will be considered Confidential Information under this agreement.'
perturbation.contradicted_text: A) that the Confidential Information shall comprise only written documents explicitly marked as 'Confidential Information' provided directly by us to you in connection with the Proposed Transaction. No oral or electronic information will be considered Confidential Information under this agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in section 7.2 introduces an exception regarding security vulnerabilities that contradicts the original, broader exclusion of liability for information entering the public domain through no fault of the receiving party. This creates ambiguity about the circumstances under which the receiving party is liable. Further, the Changed Text has wording that goes against Section A 'that the Confidential Information shall comprise only written documents explicitly marked as 'Confidential Information' provided directly by us to you in connection with the Proposed Transaction. No oral or electronic information will be considered Confidential Information under this agreement.' The contradiction arises because the initial text only covers written documents, so the receiving party having a security vulnerability doesn't cause a contradiction. However, the added section in 7.2 creates a scenario of leaked information, implying that electronic information is covered by the agreement, directly contradicting section A.

perturbation.original_text: Emails with Original Signatures (not electronic) shall be deemed valid and enforceable in respect of any provisions of this NDA (Non-Disclosure Agreement). EDT documents shall be subject to European Community Directive No. 95/46/EEC, as applicable. Please note that should you choose to return the NDA via Email, we respectfully request that the original is returned by post.
perturbation.changed_text: Emails with Original Signatures (not electronic) shall be deemed valid and enforceable in respect of any provisions of this NDA (Non-Disclosure Agreement). However, for clauses related to liability, only physically signed documents received via postal mail will be considered enforceable. EDT documents shall be subject to European Community Directive No. 95/46/EEC, as applicable. Please note that should you choose to return the NDA via Email, we respectfully request that the original is returned by post.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction about the enforceability of email signatures. The original states that emails with original (non-electronic) signatures are valid for *any* provision of the NDA. The altered text adds a caveat: for liability clauses, only physical, mailed documents are enforceable. This creates confusion and potential disputes over which signature method applies to specific breaches of the agreement. This is contradictory if the Email includes a liability claim but can be not applicable if not phisically sent.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Emails with Original Signatures (not electronic) shall be deemed valid and enforceable in respect of any provisions of this NDA (Non-Disclosure Agreement). However, for clauses related to liability, only physically signed documents received via postal mail will be considered enforceable. EDT documents shall be subject to European Community Directive No. 95/46/EEC, as applicable. Please note that should you choose to return the NDA via Email, we respectfully request that the original is returned by post.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that emails with original (non-electronic) signatures are valid for *any* provision of the NDA. The changed text adds a caveat stating that for liability clauses, only physical, mailed documents are enforceable. This directly contradicts the initial statement by creating an exception to the 'any provision' clause. This is a strong contradiction because it introduces ambiguity regarding which signature method applies to liability-related clauses, thus fitting the definition of 'Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction'.
